html:
Wolfgang Meusslin (also known as "Musculus" Meusslin), born September 10, 1497, and died August 30, 1563, was a professor of theology in Bern. He is represented in the Swedish hymnal of 1986 with one work (number 185), which was originally written in Latin in the 3rd century by Ambrose of Milan: "Christe, qui lux es et dies."

== Hymns == "O Christ, you who are the light" (number 185), translated into German from Latin in 1526. Available on Wikisource.

=== Notes ===

How certain can we be about this?

From the Vasa era onwards, individuals are well-documented in archives. The uncertainty lies more in interpretation and how later generations perceived them – especially since those in power themselves played a role in shaping historical narratives.
tldr: The text is about Wolfgang Meusslin, a theologian who lived in the 16th century, and whose translation of an ancient Christian hymn is included in the Swedish hymnal.
lattlast:
Wolfgang Meusslin was a theology teacher in the city of Bern. He lived between 1497 and 1563. He is primarily known for translating an old religious poem into German in 1526.

This poem was originally written in Latin by a man named Ambrosius of Milan in the 3rd century. The text that Meusslin translated is known as "O Christ, you who are the light" and is included in the Swedish hymn book from 1986.

Because Meusslin lived during the Vasa era, he is well-documented in historical archives. However, it can be difficult for historians to know exactly how posterity has viewed him, as power at that time often influenced how history was written.
